message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  In this paper we develop a simple model of the inhaled flow rate of aerosol particles of respiratory origin
                  <jats:italic>i.e</jats:italic>
                  . that have been exhaled by other humans. A connection is made between the exposure dose and the probability of developing an airborne disease. This allows a simple assessment of the outdoor versus indoor risk of contamination to be made in a variety of meteorological situations. It is shown quantitatively that for most cases, the outdoor risk is orders of magnitude less than the indoor risk and that it can become comparable only for extremely specific meteorological and geographical situations. It sheds a light on various observations of Covid-19 spreading in mountain valleys with temperature inversions while at the same time other areas are much less impacted.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Risk of covid-19 airborne transmission.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Quantitative assessment of outdoor versus indoor airborne risk of transmission.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Meteorological and geographical influence on covid-19 airborne transmission.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Simple quantitative assessment of the outdoor versus indoor airborne transmission of viruses and covid-19
